---
title: "Uzbekistan Expanded Higher Education. Did Equality of Opportunity Follow?"
subtitle: "Evidence from three waves of household survey data, 2010--2023"
author:
  - name: "Nozimjon Ortiqov"
    affiliation: "Center for Economic Research and Reforms"
    note: "CAP Fellow"
date: "April 2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    theme: [cosmo, styles/policy-brief.scss]
  typst:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    template-partials:
      - styles/typst-template.typ
      - styles/typst-show.typ
    fontsize: 10pt
    fig-width: 6.3
    fig-height: 4
    margin:
      top: 2.5cm
      bottom: 2.5cm
      x: 2.5cm
bibliography: []
suppress-bibliography: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup

source(file.path("..", "R", "91_manuscript_helpers.R"))
load_main_manuscript_context("..")

# Load brief values extracted by reports/scripts/build_policy_brief_pdf.R
bv <- utils::read.csv(file.path("brief_values.csv"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
bval <- function(key) bv$value[bv$key == key]

# Additional data for inline values not in brief_values.csv
hbs_support_context <- read_csv_safe(project_file("outputs", "tables", "hbs_household_support_context.csv"))

hbs_val <- function(metric, column = "national") {
  rows <- hbs_support_context[hbs_support_context$metric == metric, ]
  if (nrow(rows) == 0 || !(column %in% names(rows))) return(NA)
  as.numeric(rows[[column]][1])
}

# Rounding helpers for prose (matching PDF style: whole percents, no decimal)
rpct <- function(x) sprintf("%.0f", 100 * as.numeric(x))
```

::: {.callout-note title="SUMMARY"}
Since 2017, Uzbekistan has nearly tripled higher-education enrollment and doubled the number of institutions. This brief asks whether the expansion has made educational opportunity more equal. Drawing on three waves of the Life in Transition Survey (2010, 2016, 2022-23) covering `r format(as.numeric(bval("sample_n")), big.mark = ",")` adults aged 25--64, the answer is: not yet. A persistence score that measures how closely children's educational position tracks their parents' rose from `r bval("persistence_2010")` in 2010 to `r bval("persistence_2016")` in 2016 and stood at `r bval("persistence_2022")` in 2022-23. In the latest wave, `r rpct(as.numeric(gsub("%", "", bval("tertiary_persistence"))) / 100)` percent of respondents whose parents held a university degree also attained one, while those from less-educated families mostly stopped at upper secondary. During COVID-19 school closures, mothers provided `r rpct(as.numeric(gsub("%", "", bval("support_mother"))) / 100)` percent of learning support at home. Urban households fared better than rural ones on nearly every access measure. The evidence points to three priorities: target support where students fall behind, make the tertiary expansion equitable rather than just bigger, and build a system to track whether opportunity is becoming more equal.
:::

## The expansion is real, but uneven

Between 2019/20 and 2024/25, the number of higher education institutions in Uzbekistan rose from 119 to 222, and total enrollment grew from about 441,000 to over 1.4 million.^[Administrative higher-education series compiled from official sources. Covers all recognized higher education institutions nationwide.] This is one of the fastest tertiary expansions in the post-Soviet region.

It was not, however, uniform. The national average more than doubled, from 73 to 180 students per 1,000 youth aged 20-24, yet no region outside the capital has caught up with where Tashkent city started. @fig-expansion shows the pattern: growth was fastest in regions that started with the lowest enrollment, and all regions remain far below Tashkent.

![Regional higher-education growth, 2020/21 to 2024/25 (excluding Tashkent city). *Source: Administrative higher-education series. Grey dots show 2020/21 levels; colored dots show 2024/25 levels. Tashkent city is excluded (it grew from ~1,026 to ~2,869 students per 1,000 youth aged 20-24). Regions are colored by expansion intensity.*](../outputs/figures/fig1_regional_final.png){#fig-expansion}

How families finance this new access matters. In high-expansion regions, 26 percent of remittance-receiving households channel remittances toward education, compared with 16 percent in low-expansion regions.^[Descriptive comparison from the Household Budget Survey (2021--2025), split by regional expansion intensity. High-expansion regions: 26.1% remittance-for-education rate; low-expansion: 16.4%. Overall education spending shares are nearly identical across groups.] Families in fast-expanding areas appear to be paying for access partly through migration income rather than through stronger local economies.

Whether broader access is translating into more equal opportunity, or whether family background still determines who benefits, is the central question.

## Family background still predicts who gets ahead

Three waves of the Life in Transition Survey (LiTS) allow us to track how closely children's education follows their parents' over a 13-year period.^[The Life in Transition Survey (LiTS) is conducted by the EBRD. The 2010 and 2016 waves covered 10 oblasts; 2022-23 expanded to all 14. Analytical sample: `r format(as.numeric(bval("sample_n")), big.mark = ",")` adults aged 25--64 with non-missing own and parental education.] The key metric is a persistence score: higher values mean that children's educational rank tracks parental rank more closely. That score rose from `r bval("persistence_2010")` in 2010 to `r bval("persistence_2016")` in 2016, a statistically significant increase (p < 0.01), and stood at `r bval("persistence_2022")` in 2022-23 (@fig-persistence). The apparent decline after 2016 is not statistically significant. Part of the low 2010 reading may reflect differences in how parental education was recorded that year, so the 2010-to-2016 increase should be read with some caution.^[The 2010 LiTS wave recorded parental education in years of schooling rather than categories, and parental-education missingness was higher than in later waves. Sensitivity checks in the companion paper show that the wave ordering of persistence scores is robust to multiple imputation approaches, but the 2010 level carries more uncertainty than the 2016 or 2022-23 estimates.] Still, the overall picture is clear: family background and educational attainment remain closely linked, and the expansion has not loosened that link.

![Persistence score across LiTS waves. Higher values mean children's educational position tracks their parents' more closely. *Source: Life in Transition Survey (EBRD), waves II (2010), III (2016), and IV (2022-23). Adults aged 25--64 in Uzbekistan. Shaded band: 95% confidence interval.*](../outputs/figures/tier_a_rank_rank_by_wave.png){#fig-persistence}

Controlling for region, birth cohort, urban residence, and gender does not change this result. Parental education remains the strongest predictor of attainment in every specification. Adding migration exposure and household structure to the model barely moves the parental education coefficient.

Transition data illustrate the inequality directly. In 2022-23, `r rpct(as.numeric(gsub("%", "", bval("tertiary_persistence"))) / 100)` percent of respondents from university-educated families also reached university. Those from upper-secondary backgrounds were roughly split between staying at the same level and moving up. Rows for lower-origin families were too thin for reliable estimates, which itself reflects limited access at the bottom.

In international comparison, these persistence levels place Uzbekistan in a moderate-to-high range for developing countries, comparable to Turkey and other middle-income settings where enrollment has grown but family-background gradients have not faded.^[Hertz et al. (2008) place intergenerational education correlations between about 0.20 and 0.60 across countries. See also Narayan et al. (2018) and Aydemir and Yazici (2019).]

## Where you live and when you were born also matter

National averages hide meaningful variation. Urban residents show consistently higher upward mobility than rural residents across all three survey waves.^[Category-based upward mobility rates by urban/rural residence across all three waves. The urban advantage is descriptive; the rank-based series is the preferred cross-wave benchmark.] This gap is not simply about income: rural households actually report higher emigrant rates (`r rpct(hbs_val("has_emigrant_hh", "rural"))` vs. `r rpct(hbs_val("has_emigrant_hh", "urban"))` percent) and more remittance receipt (`r rpct(hbs_val("has_remittance_hh", "rural"))` vs. `r rpct(hbs_val("has_remittance_hh", "urban"))` percent), yet the educational mobility gap remains. Rural areas face several overlapping problems, including fewer nearby institutions, weaker local labor markets for graduates, and less reliable connectivity. Remittances do not solve these structural gaps. At the same time, if emigration is selective by family education, the survey-based estimates may not fully reflect mobility patterns among families with the highest migration rates.^[If educational selectivity of emigration differs by family background, the survey-based persistence estimates may not fully capture mobility patterns among the most mobile families. This applies to all household-survey analyses of countries with high emigration.]

Age matters as well. Younger adults surveyed (25-34) show a different trajectory from older cohorts, with persistence scores that shift more across waves. But in every age group and every wave, family background still predicts educational attainment. No cohort has escaped this pattern.

Regional variation is wide. In 2022-23, the persistence score ranged from near zero in some regions to above 0.4 in Tashkent city. Institutional density, labor market structure, and household resources all shape how much family background matters in a given place.

## COVID-19 showed what happens when schools close

The 2022-23 LiTS wave asked a subsample of about 180 households with school-age children about learning during COVID-19.^[The child-module subsample covers respondents with a child enrolled before COVID and non-missing parental schooling, about 180 households after exclusions. Results should be read as illustrative.] The disruption was large. Seventy-eight percent of households reported that a child's education stopped entirely. Three in four faced at least one barrier to remote learning: `r rpct(as.numeric(gsub("%", "", bval("challenge_internet"))) / 100)` percent reported internet problems, and `r rpct(as.numeric(gsub("%", "", bval("challenge_device"))) / 100)` percent lacked adequate devices.

When schools closed, mothers stepped in. They provided learning support in `r rpct(as.numeric(gsub("%", "", bval("support_mother"))) / 100)` percent of cases; fathers in only `r rpct(as.numeric(gsub("%", "", bval("support_father"))) / 100)` percent. A household's ability to cushion the disruption therefore depended almost entirely on the mother's education, time, and energy. Families where parents had less schooling reported more internet and device problems on top of that, reinforcing existing gaps when they mattered most.

The lesson goes beyond pandemics. Whenever learning shifts to the household, whether through school closure, teacher shortages, or infrastructure problems, families with fewer resources lose ground. Effective digital inclusion requires not just internet coverage but also working devices, reliable connections, and adults who can support learning at home.

## Household budgets confirm the uneven playing field

National Household Budget Survey data (2021--2025) provide additional context.^[Household Budget Survey 2021--2025, pooled. Descriptive context only. The parent-child linkage rate in HBS is too low for direct mobility estimation. Full methods are in the companion paper.] About `r rpct(hbs_val("education_spending_positive"))` percent of households report positive education spending. Some `r rpct(hbs_val("has_tutoring"))` percent pay for tutoring, a private investment that reflects how much the system relies on out-of-pocket supplementation. Nearly `r rpct(hbs_val("has_remittance_hh"))` percent of households receive remittances, and `r rpct(hbs_val("internet_access_hh"))` percent have internet access.

When admissions expand, better-resourced families are positioned to benefit first. In high-expansion regions, the pattern is visible: remittance-for-education spending runs 10 percentage points above low-expansion regions, while general education spending is nearly identical across the two groups. Supply is expanding, but advantages on the demand side have not changed.

## Three priorities for making expansion equitable

The evidence does not identify causes, but it points to three areas where policy could narrow the gap between expanded access and equal opportunity.

**1. Target support where students fall behind.** The data suggest that students lose ground not at the university gate but earlier, at the transitions between primary, secondary, and post-secondary levels. Diagnostic assessments at these points can catch learning gaps before they widen. Transition guidance and catch-up programs around national exam years are likely to be more effective than remediation that arrives after students have already fallen behind. Rural areas, where mobility is lower and institutional access is thinner, should be prioritized.

**2. Make the tertiary expansion equitable, not just bigger.** First-generation applicants face information gaps, weaker preparation, and unfamiliarity with the system that additional seats alone will not address. Outreach needs to start in secondary school. At entry, academic bridge programs and first-year advising can help underprepared students stay enrolled. In high-expansion regions, where enrollment has grown fastest but local institutional capacity is still developing, quality assurance and student support should grow as fast as admissions. The measure of success should be not how many students enter, but how many disadvantaged students enter, stay, and finish.

**3. Build a routine equity-monitoring system.** Uzbekistan currently has no regular mechanism for tracking whether opportunity is becoming more equal. An equity dashboard that combines survey data on parental education, learning conditions, and urban-rural differences with administrative records on enrollment, retention, and completion by region would give policymakers the feedback they currently lack. The LiTS data used here cover only three snapshots over 13 years. A system built on administrative records could provide annual updates and flag disparities as they develop.

---

*A note on what this evidence can and cannot show.* The analysis is descriptive: it documents associations, not causal effects of specific reforms. The COVID module covers a small subsample and illustrates patterns rather than proves mechanisms. These are real limitations. But they do not change the main finding: family background remains a strong predictor of educational attainment in Uzbekistan, and the ongoing expansion has not yet changed that. Full methods, diagnostics, and robustness tests are reported in the companion research paper and technical appendix.
